title: One-Way Traffic Between Site A and Site B

ticket.summary: Application team reports that a new backend service at Site B (10.50.0.0/24) can be reached from Site A (10.10.0.0/24) for the TCP handshake, but the connection then hangs and eventually times out. Curl from Site A to the Site B service returns 'Connection timed out' about 50% of the time; ping between the two sites' hosts is also intermittently failing. This started after Site B's new firewall (fw-siteb-01) was put into service yesterday.

topology.description: Site A hosts (10.10.0.0/24) reach Site B hosts (10.50.0.0/24) over a WAN backbone through two core routers: core-a1 at Site A and core-b1 at Site B, with a stateful firewall fw-siteb-01 sitting in front of the Site B server subnet. core-b1 has two equal-cost-looking paths back toward Site A: a direct WAN link to core-a1, and a secondary link via a legacy router core-b2 that is only meant to be used as a backup. A static route added on core-b1 last week for a different purpose is unintentionally preferred for return traffic to 10.10.0.0/24, sending return traffic via core-b2, which bypasses fw-siteb-01's stateful session table.

links.label: WAN path, forward traffic to Site B

logs.content:
07:41:02 NEW-CONN src=10.10.0.15 dst=10.50.0.20 dport=8443 proto=tcp state=SYN_SENT
07:41:32 SESSION-TIMEOUT src=10.10.0.15 dst=10.50.0.20 dport=8443 reason=no-return-traffic-observed
07:41:32 note: SYN-ACK from 10.50.0.20 never traversed this firewall for the matching session

Jul 13 16:02:09 core-b1 %ROUTE-6-CHANGE: static route 10.10.0.0/24 added via core-b2 (added for temp-migration-project, TTL not set)
Jul 14 07:41:02 core-b1 %FLOW: return packet src=10.50.0.20 dst=10.10.0.15 matched route 10.10.0.0/24 via core-b2 (admin distance 1) over direct-via-fw route (admin distance 110)

cli_outputs.output:
Routing entry for 10.10.0.0/24
  Known via "static", distance 1, metric 0
  Routing Descriptor Blocks:
  * via core-b2 (10.50.0.254)
      Route metric is 0, traffic share count is 1

Note: OSPF-learned route to 10.10.0.0/24 via fw-siteb-01 (10.50.0.2) exists with distance 110 but is not preferred (static distance 1 wins).

* Trying 10.50.0.20:8443...
* Connected to 10.50.0.20 port 8443
* (sending request, no response)
curl: (28) Failed to connect to 10.50.0.20 port 8443: Connection timed out
Gi0/1: up/up, description 'LEGACY BACKUP LINK - DO NOT USE FOR PRODUCTION', last config change 2024-11-02

monitoring.notes: Grafana shows TCP handshake success from Site A to the Site B service dropped from 100% to roughly 50% starting 2026-07-13 16:02, exactly when the temp-migration-project static route was added on core-b1. Failures correlate with flows whose return path selection alternates due to ECMP-like load distribution between the static route (dist 1) and the OSPF-learned route (dist 110) evaluated per new connection burst; the firewall shows a matching spike in 'no-return-traffic-observed' session timeouts starting at the same time.

hints: SYN reaches the server (per firewall logs) but the SYN-ACK never comes back through the firewall — look at the return path, not the forward path.
Check core-b1's routing table for 10.10.0.0/24 — is there more than one route, and which one is actually preferred?

A static route to 10.10.0.0/24 via core-b2 (admin distance 1) was added last week and is beating the correct OSPF route via fw-siteb-01 (admin distance 110), sending return traffic around the stateful firewall so it drops the asymmetric session.

solution.root_cause: A static route to 10.10.0.0/24 via the legacy backup router core-b2 was added on core-b1 on 2026-07-13 for an unrelated migration project. Because static routes have a lower administrative distance (1) than the OSPF-learned route through fw-siteb-01 (110), core-b1 now sends return traffic to Site A via core-b2, bypassing the stateful firewall that saw the outbound SYN, so the firewall never sees the return traffic and the session times out.
solution.fix: Remove the stray static route to 10.10.0.0/24 via core-b2 on core-b1 (or raise its administrative distance / restrict it to only failover scenarios) so return traffic follows the same path through fw-siteb-01 as the forward traffic.
